q: DLT pipeline expectations: what is the difference between EXPECT, EXPECT OR DROP, and EXPECT OR FAIL, and how do you choose?
a: Databricks Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines (formerly DLT) offers three violation modes for row-level quality rules. EXPECT (warn): failing rows are written to the target table and the violation is counted in the event log — the pipeline continues. Use this at bronze where you never want ingestion to halt; you're surfacing quality signals without blocking data flow. EXPECT OR DROP: failing rows are silently removed before the data is written to the target. Use this at silver to produce a clean, curated dataset — downstream consumers get only valid rows, and the dropped count is visible in the event log for monitoring. EXPECT OR FAIL: any failing row halts the entire pipeline update with an error. Use this at gold or for aggregates where a bad row would corrupt a metric, a report, or a downstream SLA — the pipeline should stop rather than silently produce wrong answers. The Python decorators are @dp.expect, @dp.expect_or_drop, @dp.expect_or_fail; in SQL, add CONSTRAINT name EXPECT (predicate) [ON VIOLATION DROP ROW | FAIL UPDATE]. The expect_all / expect_all_or_drop / expect_all_or_fail variants accept a dict of named constraints so you can define a reusable quality contract in one place.
fj: I'd use EXPECT OR FAIL everywhere so the pipeline always stops on bad data.
fs: Using EXPECT OR FAIL at bronze halts ingestion on the first bad source record — that's too strict. A tiered approach matches enforcement to impact: warn at bronze (never block), drop at silver (clean the curated layer), fail at gold (protect aggregates and reports). A single bad source row should not take down your whole pipeline.

code.label: Tiered expectations — bronze / silver / gold
lines: # Bronze: warn only — never block ingestion
# Silver: drop bad rows, keep clean data
# Gold: fail fast — bad aggregates are worse than no data

code.label: SQL constraint syntax for all three modes
lines: CONSTRAINT non_null_id EXPECT (id IS NOT NULL);
